title: Mathematical Theories of Interaction with Oracles: Active Property Testing and New Models for Learning Boolean Functions
speaker: Liu Yang
affiliation: School of Computer Science, Carnegie Mellon University
abstract: With the notion of interaction with oracles as a unifying theme of much of my dissertation work, I discuss novel models and results for property testing and computational learning, with the use of Fourier analytic and probabilistic methods.

proof_stages.label: Property-testing framework and membership-query motivation
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines Boolean-function property testing, its distance promise, and its role as a preprocessing step before learning, then argues that classical membership queries are often unrealistic for machine-learning labelers.

proof_stages.label: Active testing model and headline separations
proof_stages.summary: Pool-based active learning is imported into property testing by allowing many cheap unlabeled samples but only a few label queries, yielding an active-testing model positioned between passive sampling and membership-query testing and summarized by query-complexity separations.

proof_stages.label: Noise-sensitivity tester for unions of intervals
proof_stages.summary: For unions of d intervals, a local noise-sensitivity characterization shows that true interval unions have low noise sensitivity and functions far from them have noticeably higher noise sensitivity, giving a constant-label active tester and extending to general distributions via a CDF transform.

proof_stages.label: LTF testing via pairwise correlation statistics
proof_stages.summary: For linear threshold functions, the upper bound is obtained by estimating a label-angle correlation expectation, replacing a naive linear-sample estimator with all pairwise statistics from about sqrt(n) samples and applying U-statistic concentration.

proof_stages.label: Testing dimension and lower-bound consequences
proof_stages.summary: A minimax, distribution-specific testing dimension is defined through priors and total-variation distances between induced label distributions, shown to characterize passive and active query complexity, and used to derive dictator and LTF lower bounds together with remaining active-testing gaps.

proof_stages.label: Pairwise-similarity oracle model for DNF learning
proof_stages.summary: The talk introduces Boolean and numerical pairwise queries for positive examples of a DNF, then uses the extra similarity information to learn disjoint and weakly disjoint DNF formulas by graphing shared-term relations and extracting conjunctions.

proof_stages.label: Hardness via group-learning reduction
proof_stages.summary: A reduction from group learning shows that, under arbitrary distributions, Boolean pair queries and even approximate numerical pair queries do not make general DNF learning easier than standard DNF learning.

proof_stages.label: Exact numerical DNF learning under the uniform distribution
proof_stages.summary: With exact numerical pair queries under the uniform distribution, landmark positive examples reduce learning to sums of monotone terms, which are learned efficiently by a Fourier-coefficient greedy procedure, leaving open the remaining combinations of query type and distribution.

examples.name: Testing linearity of Boolean functions
examples.note: The familiar linearity-testing promise is used to make the accept/reject/gray-zone definition of property testing concrete, but it is not used in the later arguments.
examples.engagement_reason: He uses linearity testing only to illustrate the accept/reject definition for property testing, without carrying out a linearity test or computation.

passes.reason: standard linearity testing is used as a representative property-testing task, not as a structural exception or obstruction
Highly regular algebraic Boolean-function property used as a standard positive illustration.
Linearity is used as a standard illustrative Boolean-function testing property, not to refute a claim.
linearity is used as a standard property-testing benchmark, with the linear/parity class being highly regular rather than adversarial.
linear Boolean functions are highly algebraically regular characters, used here as a standard positive testing example.
standard Boolean linearity testing is used as an illustrative property-testing task, not as a special or adversarial object
linear Boolean functions form a highly regular algebraic class used as a standard testing example
Canonical linearity-testing task used only to illustrate property-testing definitions, with no special structural status singled out here.
Used as a standard positive property-testing example; linear Boolean functions form a highly regular algebraic class.

linearity is used as a standard illustrative property-testing problem, with the linear Boolean functions forming a highly regular algebraic class
A standard linear/parity-function testing problem is used as a baseline illustration.

examples.name: Artificial cat-versus-dog image query
examples.note: A computer-generated image in a cat/dog classifier illustrates why arbitrary membership queries may fall off the natural data manifold and be meaningless for a human labeler.
examples.engagement_reason: The artificial cat-versus-dog image is invoked as an unrealistic membership query that humans cannot label, with no further analysis.

passes.reason: the artificial cat/dog query is used to show membership queries can produce unlabelable out-of-distribution inputs
Off-manifold synthetic image query used to show membership queries can be unlabelable for humans.
The artificial cat/dog query is an off-distribution query vector used to show membership queries can be unanswerable by humans.
the artificial cat/dog query is used to show that arbitrary membership queries can be nonsensical for human labelers.
the artificial cat/dog image is used to show arbitrary membership queries can produce human-unlabelable off-distribution inputs.
the artificial cat-versus-dog query is used to show membership queries can produce human-unanswerable out-of-distribution instances
the artificial cat-dog query is used to show membership queries can produce unnatural unlabelable inputs
Off-manifold artificial image vector lacking natural cat/dog image structure, used to show membership queries can be nonsensical.
A nonsensical artificial image query is used to show membership queries can fail because they lack natural image structure.
The artificial cat/dog query lacks the structure of a natural image and is used to show membership queries can be meaningless for humans.
the artificial cat/dog query is used to show membership queries can be nonsensical, and the image lacks the coherent structure of natural images
An unnatural constructed image is used as an obstruction to arbitrary membership queries.

examples.name: Synthetic medical-record query
examples.note: A fabricated lab-file or medical-record instance illustrates that even formally valid feature vectors may not correspond to a real patient whom a physician can diagnose.
examples.engagement_reason: The synthetic medical-record case is described as another unrealistic generated query for a physician, but no construction or calculation is performed on it.

passes.reason: the synthetic medical record is used as an impossible/unrealistic query obstructing human oracle labeling
Physiologically inconsistent synthetic record used to obstruct the realism of membership queries.
The synthetic medical record is used as an obstruction to realistic membership queries because no real patient need correspond to it.
the synthetic medical record is an obstruction example to the realism of membership-query labeling.
the synthetic medical record is used to show membership queries may generate medically incoherent cases with no real patient to label.
the synthetic medical record is used as an obstruction to the realism of arbitrary membership queries
the synthetic medical record is used as an incoherent non-realistic input obstructing human membership-query labels

The synthetic medical record is an incoherent non-patient case used to show constructed membership queries can fail in practice.

examples.name: Movie-review bag-of-words representation
examples.note: Sentiment classification with bag-of-words vectors illustrates how a learner’s internal representation can discard structure that humans need in order to label examples reliably.
examples.engagement_reason: He takes the movie-review example, explains its bag-of-words Boolean-vector representation, and reasons through why that representation loses structure needed for human labeling.

passes.reason: bag-of-words collapses sentence order and syntax and is used to show feature-vector queries may be hard for humans to label
Order-forgetting bag-of-words vector used to show internal representations can be unlabelable despite valid source text.
The bag-of-words vector collapses sentence order and syntax and is used to show internal representations can make membership queries hard to label.
the bag-of-words vector collapses sentence order and syntax and is used to show membership queries can be hard even on valid data.
bag-of-words collapses the sentence’s order and syntax, and is used to show valid internal representations can still defeat human labeling.
the bag-of-words vector collapses a review by discarding order and syntax, illustrating why internal representations can make membership queries hard for humans
the bag-of-words vector is a collapsed text representation used to show internal feature queries may be hard for humans to label
Orderless bag-of-words vector collapses sentence syntax and is used to show valid internal queries may still be hard to label.
The bag-of-words vector forgets word order and syntax and is used to show even in-support membership queries may be unlabelable.

the bag-of-words movie-review vector is used to expose a failure mode of membership queries and is a collapsed representation with word-order structure removed
The order-forgetting bag-of-words vector is a collapsed representation used to expose a labeling failure mode.

examples.name: Birthday-paradox close-pair analogy
examples.note: The birthday-paradox intuition explains why passive sampling needs many labeled examples before seeing nearby pairs, while active testing can search a cheap unlabeled pool for such pairs.
examples.engagement_reason: The birthday paradox is cited only as an analogy for needing about square-root-many passive samples to see a close pair.

passes.reason: the birthday-paradox threshold is a critical collision bound used as an obstruction to passive close-pair sampling
Critical birthday-threshold close-pair phenomenon used as the passive-model obstruction to few-sample local comparisons.
The birthday-paradox close-pair phenomenon is invoked as the obstruction forcing many passive samples before local comparisons are possible.
the birthday-paradox close-pair threshold is a critical square-root sampling phenomenon used to obstruct passive close-pair testing.
the birthday-paradox close-pair threshold is the critical sqrt-size collision scale and is used as an obstruction to passive local testing.
the birthday-paradox close-pair threshold is a critical square-root boundary used to obstruct low-query passive testing
the birthday-paradox close-pair phenomenon is invoked as a sharp sampling-threshold analogy rather than a refuting example
Critical birthday-paradox close-pair threshold supplies the passive-testing obstruction to getting local pairs with few labels.
The birthday-paradox close-pair threshold is a critical sample-size boundary used to argue passive testing cannot cheaply find close pairs.
The birthday-paradox close-pair threshold is invoked as the tight obstruction showing passive samples need about sqrt(d) before nearby pairs appear.
the birthday-paradox close-pair phenomenon is invoked as a lower-bound obstruction for passive sampling and concerns the critical collision threshold around square-root sample size
The birthday-paradox phenomenon gives the critical square-root close-pair threshold obstructing passive testing.

examples.name: Dictator functions as a testing-dimension lower-bound case
examples.note: Uniform random dictators versus random Boolean functions illustrate how the testing-dimension framework proves that small samples induce nearly indistinguishable label distributions.
examples.engagement_reason: He uses dictator functions in the testing-dimension framework, chooses explicit priors, and reasons why their induced label distributions remain close up to order log n samples.

passes.reason: dictators are minimal one-coordinate Boolean functions used as a lower-bound enemy case for testing dimension
Minimal one-coordinate Boolean functions used as adversarial testing-dimension lower-bound instances.
Dictator functions are minimal one-coordinate Boolean functions used as an adversarial lower-bound case for testing dimension.
dictators are minimal one-coordinate Boolean functions used as an adversarial lower-bound family for testing dimension.
dictators are minimal one-coordinate Boolean functions, used as a hard lower-bound case requiring logarithmically many queries.
dictator functions are one-coordinate projection base cases used to force a logarithmic active/passive testing lower bound
dictator functions are minimal one-coordinate Boolean functions used as an adversarial lower-bound case for testing dimension
One-coordinate minimal junta is used as an adversarial testing-dimension lower-bound instance requiring logarithmic queries.

Dictators are minimal nonconstant one-coordinate Boolean functions used as an adversarial lower-bound family requiring logarithmically many queries.

examples.name: Disjoint DNF warm-up
examples.note: The disjoint-DNF special case reveals the key pairwise-query idea—partition positive examples by the term they satisfy, learn each conjunction, and OR the results—which motivates the weakly disjoint algorithm.
examples.engagement_reason: He runs the similarity-query learning idea on disjoint DNF by partitioning positives by term, learning each conjunction by intersection, and OR-ing the results.

passes.reason: disjoint DNF is the zero-overlap boundary case used only as an easy positive warm-up
Zero-overlap DNF base case used as an easy positive warm-up for the new queries.
Disjoint DNF is the zero-overlap boundary case with no term interactions, used only as an easy positive warm-up.
disjoint DNF is the zero-overlap boundary case of DNF formulas, used only as an easy positive warm-up.
disjoint DNF is the zero-overlap base case of DNF learning, used as an easy positive warm-up rather than a refutation.
disjoint DNF removes overlap between terms, making it a simplified warm-up positive case rather than an adversarial example
